prompt: A portfolio manager is asked to report AI progress to the steering committee. Which of these is a vanity metric that should NOT be the headline?
choices: Hours saved per analyst across the live initiatives
Support tickets resolved without a human, against the agreed target
The total number of AI pilots the organisation is running
Adoption: active users and repeat use of the shipped features
explanation: The number of pilots measures activity, not outcome — a classic vanity metric. Hours saved, tickets auto-resolved against target, and real adoption all tie to business value and belong in the portfolio view; a pilot count does not.

prompt: Two pilots in your portfolio independently need the same eval harness, guardrail/PII checks, and cost observability. From a portfolio standpoint, what is usually the better investment? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Fund the capability once as a shared platform both pilots adopt
Let each team rebuild its own copy so they stay fully independent
A shared platform also lowers the cost and risk of future initiatives that need the same capability
Building it twice is fine because duplicated plumbing has no downside
explanation: When several initiatives need the same capability, funding it once as a shared platform avoids paying twice and makes every current and future pilot cheaper and more consistent. Deliberately rebuilding the same plumbing per team is waste and produces inconsistent risk controls.

prompt: A pilot has already consumed a large share of its budget but still shows no value against its agreed metric. What is the disciplined portfolio decision, and why?
choices: Keep funding it because stopping now would waste what has already been spent
Kill or pause it; the decision should weigh future return, not sunk cost, and the freed budget can fund a better bet
Double its budget so it finally has enough to succeed
Quietly keep it running so the team's morale isn't affected
explanation: Already-spent money is sunk and irrelevant to the forward decision. If a pilot shows no value against its metric, killing or pausing it frees budget for better bets; continuing because of past cost is the sunk-cost trap that quietly drains a portfolio.

prompt: Why should a portfolio manager treat standardising every initiative on a single model provider as a risk to manage?
choices: Because using one provider is always cheaper, so there is never any risk
Because a single price change, outage, or model deprecation would then hit the whole portfolio at once — concentration risk best contained by a provider abstraction and a deliberate exit strategy
Because providers legally require you to use at least three vendors
Because model quality is identical across all providers, making the choice irrelevant
explanation: If the whole portfolio quietly depends on one provider, any price hike, outage, or version deprecation hits everything simultaneously — that's concentration risk. A provider abstraction, knowledge of switching costs, and a deliberate exit strategy keep it from becoming a portfolio-wide crisis.
